en.extract:
Gurney's sugarbird (Promerops gurneyi) is a medium-sized passerine endemic to the mid- and high-altitude grassland velds in southern Africa. It belongs to the family Promeropidae, which contains one genus, Promerops, and two species. Gurney's sugarbird feeds on nectar from Protea bushes as well as on small insects. This bird is characterized by its long, graduated tail and decurved beak.
The common name and Latin binomial commemorate the British banker and amateur ornithologist John Henry Gurney (1819-1890).  


Description
Gurney's sugarbird has a long, decurved bill characteristic of nectarivores. It has a chestnut-coloured forehead, along with a faint white eyestripe and white mustachial stripe. A white throat stands out against the russet breast. This species of sugarbird has a dark grey back, and a conspicuous bright yellow rump makes this bird easy to spot. The tail is long and graduated, ranging from 11 to 19.3 cm in length. This species measures between 23 and 29 cm in total, and body mass ranges from 30 to 46.5 g in males and 23 to 43 g in females. Females are smaller than males, with shorter bills and tail feathers. Males also have triangular-shaped bulge on the sixth primary feather, used in mating displays. Immature individuals are duller than adults and have a greenish tinge to both their breast and yellow rump. Further, their secondary feathers are edged with a brownish colour.
The two subspecies of Gurney's surgarbird have minor differences in their appearance. P. g. gurneyi individuals fit the above description. Meanwhile, P. g. ardens individuals tend to be darker, with a greener rump and a richer chestnut breast than their counterpart subspecies.
Gurney's sugarbirds moult their flight feathers (primary and secondary feathers) during the breeding season. Tail feathers, however, moult throughout the year continuously. This is because the long feathers are easily damaged and must be replaced quickly due to their important role in mating displays.


Taxonomy
Promerops gurneyi is one of two species within the genus Promerops and the monotypic family Promeropidae. Gurney's sugarbird has a single sister species, the Cape sugarbird (Promerops cafer). 
Its taxonomic ranking baffled scientists for many years. This family has been grouped in with the Nectariniidae (sunbird) family, due to their physical resemblances and shared geographic origins, as both sunbirds and sugarbirds evolved in Africa. Promeropidae was also once classified in the Australian Meliphagidae (honeyeaters) family, because of their shared and unique tongue structure, behaviour, and nest-building. There was no evidence of common lineage, however, and similarities appear to be from convergent evolution. Analysis of protein structure from egg whites and red blood cells showed that sugarbirds are derived specialists of African starlings. Genetically, the Promerops genus is most closely related to the Cinnyricinclus and Onychognathus genera. It is today classified as its own family.
Presently 2 subspecies of Gurney's sugarbird are recognized, Promerops gurneyi gurneyi and Promerops gurneyi ardens. P. g. ardens was recognized in 1952 and arose from an isolated population of Gurney's sugarbird located in high-elevation areas of Eastern Zimbabwe and the adjacent Mozambique. The ranges of the two subspecies do not overlap, and they occupy different types of habitats.


Distribution and habitat
Gurney's sugarbird is a species endemic to Southern Africa. The distribution is fragmented, and these birds are mainly found in elevated regions in north-eastern Mountain Sourveld and north-eastern Sandy Highveld in the Transvaal and Natal regions. They are also found in Highland and Dohne Sourvelds of Natal and into the Eastern Cape and Limpopo provinces. An isolated population of P. g. ardens is located in the highlands of Eastern Zimbabwe and adjacent Mozambique. Gurney's sugarbirds are short-distance altitudinal migrants and leave their breeding grounds once the flowering season is over.
The preferred habitats is shrubland velds of Southern Africa, dominated by sugarbush (Protea) species of flowering plants. Gurney's sugarbird distribution overlaps silver sugarbush (Protea roupelliae), white protea (P. subvestita), and common sugarbush (P. afra). Because this species is only associated to Protea shrubs, it is very vulnerable to habitat loss and displacement due to fires. The distribution of these birds is restrained by their very specialized diet of sugarbush species' nectar. These birds also feed on nectar from Aloe gardens in suburban areas.
P. g. ardens evolved in an area that lacks silver sugarbush, and it relies on miombo trees (Brachystegia genus) and African protea (P. gageudi), Nyanaga protea (P. dracomontana), beard protea (P. crintita), and common sugarbush (P. afra) species as food resources instead. P. g. ardens lives in riparian woods, on dry slopes with many flowering shrubs such as sugarbush (Protea), bird of paradise flowers (Strelitzia), and coral trees (Erythrina).


Behaviour


Vocalizations
The song for this species consists of three or four ascending notes, with the final note repeated several times. A "song-spell", songs repeated for an extended period of time, can last up to four minutes. Multiple song-spells strung together and separated by short pauses are called full song periods, and these can last for 20 minutes. 

Gurney's sugarbird calls are short, and characterized by a single note, often described as a "chit" sound. The call is more low-pitched than the song, and can be compounded into a "chit-chit-chit" sequence. The call is mostly used when approaching the nest, or as a indicator of well-being once a partner returns to the nest with food, for example. When defending the nest or territory from other birds, Gurney's sugarbird will make a "cloth-ripping" sound. 


Diet
Gurney's sugarbirds are specialized nectarivores and insectivores feeding on Protea species, mainly Protea roupelliae, as well as Protea afra and Aloe in suburban areas. The long, decurved bill is well-adapted to siphon nectar from Protea flower heads. These birds forage for insects and nectar within their defended territory. Insects are usually found hiding within the Protea roupelliae flowers, or caught mid-flight. Main prey orders include Hymenoptera (wasps, bees, sawflies and ants), Coleoptera (beetles), and Hemiptera (true bugs). Adults tend to feed mainly on nectar, and during the breeding season small insects are the main prey items fed to chicks.


Reproduction
Gurney's sugarbird is a socially monogamous species, meaning that pairs remain together through the breeding season. This season lasts from September, when pairing and nest-building take place, until late February, when the fledglings leave their parents' territory. Females chose their mate based on morphological features (physical attributes) and display flights, which consist of flicking their long tail and the wing-'clacking' of the triangular bulge on their sixth primary feather. Longer tails and larger feather bulges for wing-'clacking' have been associated with greater mating success in males. Display flights also take place when the males chase away encroaching individuals, usually male, from their territory.  
Reproductive success in Gurney's sugarbird is highly dependent upon available resources, such as abundant nectar and insects. Males will typically defend these resources, while the females build the nest, incubate the eggs, and feed the nestlings. Clutch size is small (usually 1-2 eggs) and is associated to this species' longer life span (up to seven years, maximum recorded longevity of 13 years). Individuals usually reach sexual maturity in their second or third year of life, and therefore typically survive for an average of five breeding seasons during their lifetime. Incubation lasts 16-17 days and brooding (keeping the chicks warm after hatching) between 19 and 23 days. Both parents participate in the feeding of the nestlings, usually bringing back small winged insects and beetles. Chicks are reliant on their parents for food for the first month of their life.


Gallery


References


External links
Gurney's sugarbird - Species text in The Atlas of Southern African Birds.
Gurney's sugarbird vocalizations
Gurney's sugarbird - Bird Life International
Gurney's sugarbird Structured guide to the species in southern Africa

'''Gurney's sugarbird''' ('''''Promerops gurneyi''''') is a medium-sized passerine [[Endemism|endemic]] to the mid- and high-altitude grassland [[Veld|velds]] in southern Africa.<ref name=":3" /> It belongs to the family [[Sugarbird|Promeropidae]], which contains one genus, ''[[Promerops]]'', and two species.<ref name=":0" /> Gurney's sugarbird feeds on nectar from ''[[Protea]]'' bushes as well as on small insects.<ref name=":0" /> This bird is characterized by its long, graduated tail and decurved beak.<ref name=":3" />

The common name and Latin binomial commemorate the British banker and amateur ornithologist [[John Henry Gurney]] (1819-1890).  

== Description ==
Gurney's sugarbird has a long, decurved bill characteristic of [[Nectarivore|nectarivores]]. It has a chestnut-coloured forehead, along with a faint white eyestripe and white mustachial stripe.<ref name=":3" /> A white throat stands out against the russet breast.<ref name=":3" /> This species of sugarbird has a dark grey back, and a conspicuous bright yellow rump makes this bird easy to spot.<ref name=":3" /> The tail is long and graduated, ranging from 11 to 19.3 cm in length.<ref name=":3" /> This species measures between 23 and 29 cm in total, and body mass ranges from 30 to 46.5 g in males and 23 to 43 g in females.<ref name=":3" /> Females are smaller than males, with shorter bills and tail feathers.<ref name=":6" /> Males also have triangular-shaped bulge on the sixth [[Flight feather|primary feather]], used in mating displays.<ref name=":1" /> Immature individuals are duller than adults and have a greenish tinge to both their breast and yellow rump.<ref name=":3" /> Further, their [[Flight feather|secondary feathers]] are edged with a brownish colour.<ref name=":3" />

The two [[subspecies]] of Gurney's surgarbird have minor differences in their appearance. ''P. g. gurneyi'' individuals fit the above description. Meanwhile, ''P. g. ardens'' individuals tend to be darker, with a greener rump and a richer chestnut breast than their counterpart subspecies.<ref name=":3" />

Gurney's sugarbirds moult their [[Flight feather|flight feathers]] (primary and secondary feathers) during the breeding season. Tail feathers, however, moult throughout the year continuously. This is because the long feathers are easily damaged and must be replaced quickly due to their important role in mating displays.<ref name=":7" />

== Taxonomy ==
''Promerops gurneyi'' is one of two species within the genus ''Promerops'' and the [[Monotypic taxon|monotypic family]] [[Sugarbird|Promeropidae]]. Gurney's sugarbird has a single sister species, the [[Cape sugarbird]] (''Promerops cafer''). 

Its taxonomic ranking baffled scientists for many years. This family has been grouped in with the [[Sunbird|Nectariniidae]] (sunbird) family, due to their physical resemblances and shared geographic origins, as both sunbirds and sugarbirds evolved in Africa. [[Sugarbird|Promeropidae]] was also once classified in the Australian [[Honeyeater|Meliphagidae]] (honeyeaters) family, because of their shared and unique tongue structure, behaviour, and nest-building. There was no evidence of common lineage, however, and similarities appear to be from [[convergent evolution]]. Analysis of protein structure from egg whites and red blood cells showed that sugarbirds are derived specialists of African [[Starling|starlings]]. Genetically, the [[Sugarbird|''Promerops'']] genus is most closely related to the [[Violet-backed starling|''Cinnyricinclus'']] and ''[[Onychognathus]]'' genera. It is today classified as its own family.<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal|last1=Sibley|first1=Charles G.|last2=Ahlquist|first2=Jon E.|title=The Relationships of the African Sugarbirds (Promerops) |date=1974|url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/00306525.1974.9639172|journal=Ostrich|language=en|volume=45|issue=1|pages=22–30|doi=10.1080/00306525.1974.9639172|issn=0030-6525|url-access=subscription}}</ref>

Presently 2 subspecies of Gurney's sugarbird are recognized, ''Promerops gurneyi gurneyi'' and ''Promerops gurneyi ardens''.<ref name=":3" /> ''P. g. ardens'' was recognized in 1952<ref name=":6">{{Cite web|title=ITIS Standard Report Page: Promerops gurneyi|url=https://www.itis.gov/servlet/SingleRpt/SingleRpt?search_topic=TSN&search_value=562478#null|access-date=2020-10-05|website=www.itis.gov}}</ref> and arose from an isolated population of Gurney's sugarbird located in high-elevation areas of [[Eastern Highlands|Eastern Zimbabwe]] and the adjacent [[Mozambique]].<ref name=":7">{{Cite journal|last=de Swardt|first=D. H.|title=Distribution, Biometrics and Moult of Gurney's Sugarbird Promerops Gurneyi |date=1992|url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/00306525.1992.9634176|journal=Ostrich|language=en|volume=63|issue=1|pages=13–20|doi=10.1080/00306525.1992.9634176|issn=0030-6525|url-access=subscription}}</ref> The ranges of the two subspecies do not overlap, and they occupy different types of habitats.<ref name=":3" />

== Distribution and habitat ==
Gurney's sugarbird is a species [[Endemism|endemic]] to [[Southern Africa]].<ref name=":3" /> The distribution is fragmented, and these birds are mainly found in elevated regions in north-eastern Mountain Sourveld and north-eastern Sandy [[Highveld]] in the [[Transvaal (province)|Transvaal]] and Natal regions.<ref name=":7" /> They are also found in [[Highland]] and Dohne Sourvelds of Natal and into the [[Eastern Cape]]<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":7" /> and [[Limpopo]] provinces.<ref name=":3" /> An isolated population of ''P. g. ardens'' is located in the highlands of [[Eastern Highlands|Eastern Zimbabwe]] and adjacent [[Mozambique]].<ref name=":7" /> Gurney's sugarbirds are short-distance [[Altitudinal migration|altitudinal migrants]] and leave their breeding grounds once the flowering season is over.<ref name=":3" />

The preferred habitats is shrubland [[Veld|velds]] of [[Southern Africa]], dominated by sugarbush (''[[Protea]]'') species of flowering plants.<ref name=":7" /> Gurney's sugarbird distribution overlaps silver sugarbush (''[[Protea roupelliae]])'', white protea (''P. subvestita''), and common sugarbush (''[[Protea afra|P. afra]])''.<ref name=":7" /> Because this species is only associated to ''[[Protea]]'' shrubs, it is very vulnerable to habitat loss and displacement due to fires.<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":7" /> The distribution of these birds is restrained by their very specialized diet of [[Protea|sugarbush]] species' nectar.<ref name=":7" /> These birds also feed on nectar from ''[[Aloe]]'' gardens in suburban areas.<ref name=":3" />

''P. g. ardens'' evolved in an area that lacks silver [[Protea roupelliae|sugarbush]], and it relies on miombo trees (''[[Brachystegia]]'' genus) and African protea (''[[Protea gaguedi|P. gageudi]])'', Nyanaga protea (''[[Protea dracomontana|P. dracomontana]]''), ''beard protea (''P. crintita'')'', and common sugarbush (''[[Protea afra|P. afra]]'') species as food resources instead.<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":5" /> ''P. g. ardens'' lives in riparian woods, on dry slopes with many flowering shrubs such as sugarbush (''[[Protea]])'', bird of paradise flowers (''[[Strelitzia]])'', and coral trees (''[[Erythrina]])''.<ref name=":3" />

== Behaviour ==

=== Vocalizations ===
The song for this species consists of three or four ascending notes, with the final note repeated several times. A "song-spell", songs repeated for an extended period of time, can last up to four minutes. Multiple song-spells strung together and separated by short pauses are called full song periods, and these can last for 20 minutes.<ref name=":4">{{Cite journal|last=Skead|first=David M.|title=Gurney's Sugarbird, Promerops Gurneyi Verreaux, in the Natal Drakensberg |date=1963|url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/00306525.1963.9639417|journal=Ostrich|language=en|volume=34|issue=3|pages=160–164|doi=10.1080/00306525.1963.9639417|issn=0030-6525|url-access=subscription}}</ref> 
{{Birdsong|species = ''Promerops gurneyi''|url = https://www.xeno-canto.org/species/Promerops-gurneyi}}

Gurney's sugarbird calls are short, and characterized by a single note, often described as a "chit" sound. The call is more low-pitched than the song, and can be compounded into a "chit-chit-chit" sequence. The call is mostly used when approaching the nest, or as a indicator of well-being once a partner returns to the nest with food, for example. When defending the nest or territory from other birds, Gurney's sugarbird will make a "cloth-ripping" sound.<ref name=":4" /> 

=== Diet ===
Gurney's sugarbirds are specialized [[Nectarivore|nectarivores]] and [[Insectivore|insectivores]] feeding on ''[[Protea]]'' species, mainly ''[[Protea roupelliae]],'' as well as ''[[Protea afra]]'' and ''[[Aloe]]'' in suburban areas.<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":7" /> The long, decurved bill is well-adapted to siphon nectar from ''[[Protea]]'' flower heads.<ref name=":3" /> These birds forage for insects and nectar within their defended territory.<ref name=":0" /> Insects are usually found hiding within the ''[[Protea roupelliae]]'' flowers, or caught mid-flight.<ref name=":0" /> Main prey orders include [[Hymenoptera]] ([[Wasp|wasps]], [[Bee|bees]], [[Sawfly|sawflies]] and [[Ant|ants]]), [[Beetle|Coleoptera]] (beetles), and [[Hemiptera]] (true bugs).<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=de Swardt|first1=D.H.|last2=Louw|first2=S.|date=1994|title=The diet and foraging behaviour of Gurney's sugarbird, Promerops gurneyi|journal=Navorsinge van die Nasionale Museum (Bloemfontein)}}</ref> Adults tend to feed mainly on nectar, and during the breeding season small insects are the main prey items fed to chicks.<ref name=":0" />

=== Reproduction ===
Gurney's sugarbird is a socially [[Monogamy in animals|monogamous]] species, meaning that pairs remain together through the breeding season.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last1=Calf|first1=K.M.|last2=Downs|first2=C.T.|date=2002|title=The breeding biology of Gurney's Sugarbird, Promerops gurneyi , in Qwaqwa National Park, Free State|url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.2989/00306520209485344|journal=Ostrich|language=en|volume=73|issue=1–2|pages=1–4|doi=10.2989/00306520209485344|s2cid=86319915 |issn=0030-6525|url-access=subscription}}</ref> This season lasts from September, when pairing and nest-building take place, until late February, when the fledglings leave their parents' territory.<ref name=":0" /> Females chose their mate based on morphological features (physical attributes) and display flights, which consist of flicking their long tail and the wing-'clacking' of the triangular bulge on their sixth primary feather.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last1=Calf|first1=K.M.|last2=Downs|first2=C.T|last3=Cherry|first3=M.I.|date=2001|title=Territoriality and breeding success in Gurney's sugarbird, Promerops gurneyi|url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15627020.2001.11657137|journal=African Zoology|language=en|volume=36|issue=2|pages=189–195|doi=10.1080/15627020.2001.11657137|s2cid=86391093 |issn=1562-7020|url-access=subscription}}</ref> Longer tails and larger feather bulges for wing-'clacking' have been associated with greater mating success in males.<ref name=":1" /> Display flights also take place when the males chase away encroaching individuals, usually male, from their territory.<ref name=":1" />  

Reproductive success in Gurney's sugarbird is highly dependent upon available resources, such as abundant nectar and insects.<ref name=":0" /> Males will typically defend these resources,<ref name=":1" /> while the females build the nest, incubate the eggs, and feed the nestlings.<ref name=":0" /> [[Clutch (eggs)|Clutch size]] is small (usually 1-2 eggs) and is associated to this species' longer life span<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal|last1=de Swardt|first1=D.H.|last2=Peach|first2=Will J.|date=2001|title=Annual survival of Gurney's Sugarbird, Promerops gurneyi|url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.2989/00306520109485321|journal=Ostrich|language=en|volume=72|issue=3–4|pages=206–208|doi=10.2989/00306520109485321|s2cid=83675299 |issn=0030-6525|url-access=subscription}}</ref> (up to seven years, maximum recorded longevity of 13 years<ref name=":3">{{Citation|last1=de Swardt|first1=Dawie|title=Gurney's Sugarbird (Promerops gurneyi)|date=2020-03-04|work=Birds of the World|publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology|last2=Sharpe|first2=Chris|editor-first1=Josep |editor-first2=Andrew |editor-first3=Jordi |editor-first4=David |editor-first5=Eduardo |editor-last1=Del Hoyo |editor-last2=Elliott |editor-last3=Sargatal |editor-last4=Christie |editor-last5=De Juana |doi=10.2173/bow.gursug1.01 |s2cid=216349804 }}</ref>). Individuals usually reach sexual maturity in their second or third year of life, and therefore typically survive for an average of five breeding seasons during their lifetime.<ref name=":2" /> [[Egg incubation|Incubation]] lasts 16-17 days and brooding (keeping the chicks warm after hatching) between 19 and 23 days.<ref name=":3" /> Both parents participate in the feeding of the nestlings, usually bringing back small winged insects and beetles.<ref name=":0" /> Chicks are reliant on their parents for food for the first month of their life.<ref name=":0" />
